results.title_inferred: On the Root of Mechoacán
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXVI
De la raíz de Mechoacán

La planta que produce esta raíz es de género de bejucos; llámase en la provincia de Mechoacán, donde nace más copiosamente, tachuache, aunque también se halla en otras partes. Echa una raíz larga, gruesa y que mana leche, y della nacen los tallos o vástagos, que son delgados y volubles. La hoja es como la palma de la mano y de figura de corazón; echa unas flores de forma de campanillas, largas, de un color encarnado amortiguado; su fruta es semejante a un pepino en el tamaño y hechura, con un vello blanco, y está llena de una semilla blanca, pequeña y anchuela. La raíz, cortada en tajadas, la secan y se lleva a varias partes, y es admirable purga para evacuar la flema. Hácense con ella otras muchas curas; y el nombre con que es conocida en todas las Indias es de raíz de Mechoacán.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXVI
On the Root of Mechoacán

The plant that produces this root belongs to the family of climbing vines [bejucos]; in the province of Mechoacán, where it grows most abundantly, it is called tachuache, though it is also found in other regions. It puts forth a long, thick root that exudes a milky sap, from which grow slender, twining stalks or shoots. The leaf is roughly the size of the palm of a hand and heart-shaped; it produces flowers in the form of long bell-shapes [campanillas] of a muted crimson color. Its fruit resembles a cucumber in size and form, covered with white down, and is filled with small, somewhat broad white seeds. The root is cut into slices, dried, and carried to various places; it is a remarkable purgative [purga] for evacuating phlegm [flema]. Many other cures are also effected with it; and the name by which it is known throughout all the Indies is the root of Mechoacán.

entities_mentioned.name: raíz de Mechoacán / tachuache
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ipomoea purga (Wender.) Hayne (syn. Exogonium purga), Mechoacán jalap root; a species of morning glory in the family Convolvulaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — a milky-sapped climbing vine with heart-shaped leaves, bell-shaped reddish flowers, cucumber-like fruit with white-downed exterior, and a thick root sliced and dried for use as a purgative — closely matches Ipomoea purga, the source of Mechoacán jalap, a drug widely traded in early modern European and colonial pharmacopeias. The local Purépecha/Nahuatl name 'tachuache' is recorded by other contemporaneous sources. Condition treated: excess phlegm [flema], consistent with humoral medicine; also used for broader purgative and cathartic purposes. Preparation: root sliced, dried, then administered as a purgative.

results.translator_notes: OCR errors corrected: 'lu' → 'la' (title line), 'tacJiuache' → 'tachuache', 'admirahlé' → 'admirable', 'Mechoacán' final line rendered with ligature artifact 'Me*choacán' → 'Mechoacán'. The term 'volubles' in botanical context means twining or voluble (wrapping around supports), preserved as 'twining' in translation. 'Campanillas' literally means 'little bells' and is a common Spanish descriptor for morning-glory-type flowers; retained in brackets. 'Anchuela' is a diminutive of 'ancha' (broad/wide), here describing seed shape.

results.title_inferred: On the Guadua Bamboo and the Ipa Cane (Chapters 82–83)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXII
De la guadgua

Las diferencias de cañas que nacen en este Nuevo Mundo no conocidas en España son muchas, entre las cuales no se halló la caña común de Europa, hasta que la trajeron los españoles; solamente haré aquí mención de las que yo he visto y que sirven a los moradores destas tierras en varios usos. Déstas es la guadgua, la mayor, a la cual llaman en este reino los españoles caña de Guayaquil, por traerse de aquella provincia todas las que se gastan en esta ciudad de Lima y en otras muchas partes deste reino. Es, pues, la guadgua parecida a la caña común en el color y talle, pero muy diferente en su hoja y grandeza; porque es tan gruesa, que por partes tiene de ruedo tres palmos y por en medio más de dos. Los cañutos son ordinariamente largos un codo, y algunos más, y como se van acercando al tronco y raíz, van siendo más cortos, aunque más gruesos, y el casco tiene un dedo de grueso. Crecen muy derechas estas cañas, y desde la mitad para arriba son muy copadas respecto de las muchas ramas y hojas que echan. Las ramas nacen de los ñudos, son cortas y del grosor de un dedo; de los ñudos destas ramas nacen otras muy delgadas, con que vienen a hacer la guadgua una copa prolongada y vistosa como de ciprés. Están armadas sus ramas de unas espinas más duras y recias que las del naranjo; la hoja es semejante en la hechura y tamaño a la del saúco, un poco más larga, y en el color y lustre se parece a la hoja de la caña.

Hállase dentro de cada cañuto tanta cantidad de agua cuanto basta a satisfacer la sed de dos hombres; por donde, quien se halla sediento en la montaña donde hay de estas cañas, suele barrenar o cortar un cañuto y beber del agua que sale dél, la cual, no sólo es buena y sabrosa, sino medicinal y útil contra la piedra. Cabe en cada uno destos cañutos un gran cangilón de agua, y en la provincia de Guayaquil sirven de cántaros con que van por agua a la fuente. Son tan altas las guadguas, que igualan y sobrepujan a los más altos árboles de la montaña; y así, cuando se cortan para los edificios y demás usos en que sirven, con quitarles más de la mitad que no es de provecho, parte hacia el cogollo y parte hacia el tronco, quedan tan largas que tienen a treinta y a cuarenta pies cada una; y las puntas de las que así se han cortado, son gruesas dos palmos.

Sirven estas cañas de vigas en los edificios pobres, y las rajas o listas que dellas se sacan, de travesanos; y por ser tan largas y livianas, se suelen hacer con ellas los andamios para las fábricas de los edificios; y cuando el edificio ha subido muy alto, se atan unas cañas con otras, y hincadas en tierra, sustentan en pie derecho los andamios por mucho que suba la fábrica, como vimos en esta ciudad de Lima que se hacía cuando se edificaban las torres de la Catedral, que las cañas hincadas en el suelo e injeridas unas con otras, sustentaban los andamios del remate de las torres. Hácense también muy altas y livianas escaleras de dos cañas déstas con los escalones y travesanos de listas de cuatro dedos de ancho de las mismas cañas; y sobre dos dellas asienta una litera que cargan dos mulas; y en estas literas se camina con mucho descanso por los Llanos y arenales deste reino desde el puerto de Payta hasta esta ciudad de Lima, que son doscientas leguas; y desde aquí al puerto de Arica, otras doscientas. Llámase esta planta en la diócesis de Quito, adonde se comprende la provincia de Guayaquil, guadgua; en la lengua quichua, mamac; y en aimará tiene dos nombres, que son: tupa y tocara.

CAPITULO LXXXIII
De la ipa

La ipa es una especie de caña poco más delgada que la guadgua, pero maciza y muy recia; nace en tierra húmeda, como la guadgua, y crece cinco o seis estados en alto. Las mayores son tan gruesas como el brazo, y las más delgadas, como el dedo pulgar; son más gruesas por los ñudos que por en medio de los cañutos; echan por cada ñudo un manojo muy copado de ramillos tan delgados como la caña del trigo hasta dos palmos de largo, con muchas hojas que son como las de la guadgua, un poco más angostas. Son estas cañas, cuando secas, pardiscas, y cuando verdes, muy correosas y flexibles; de suerte que con el peso de su cumbre y ramas se encorvan y doblan hasta llegar con los pimpollos a la tierra, enlazándose unas con otras de tal modo, que se hacen dellas vistosos arcos en la montaña donde las hay, que parecen hechos por industria de hombres. Nacen tan espesas, que no se puede hender por ellas. Son de tan agradable vista, que las suelen traer verdes con sus ramas y hojas a los pueblos, para enramar con ellas las calles y hacer arcos por donde pasan las procesiones en las fiestas solemnes. Llámase esta caña, en la lengua quichua, ipa, y en aimará curcuma, y también chinchiru.

Hállase otra suerte destas cañas macizas tan gruesas como las guadguas; porque me acuerdo haber visto una vez, que una destas cañas servía de puente para pasar de tierra a una fragata que estaba surta muy arrimada a ella.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXII
On the Guadua

The varieties of canes [cañas] that grow in this New World and are unknown in Spain are many; among them, the common cane of Europe was not found until the Spaniards brought it over. I shall here mention only those that I myself have seen and that serve the inhabitants of these lands in various ways. Of these, the guadua is the largest, and the Spaniards of this kingdom call it the cane of Guayaquil, because all those consumed in this city of Lima and in many other parts of this kingdom are brought from that province. The guadua resembles the common cane in color and form, but differs greatly in its leaf and size, for it is so thick that in some parts its circumference [ruedo] measures three handspans [palmos], and at its mid-section more than two. The internodes [cañutos] are ordinarily one cubit [codo] long, and some longer; as they approach the trunk and root they become shorter, though thicker, and the wall [casco] of the cane is a finger's breadth thick. These canes grow very straight, and from the midpoint upward they are very leafy, on account of the many branches and leaves they put forth. The branches spring from the nodes [ñudos], are short and a finger's breadth in diameter; from the nodes of these branches spring other very slender ones, so that the guadua comes to form a tall, handsome crown [copa] like that of a cypress. Its branches are armed with thorns harder and stronger than those of the orange tree; the leaf is similar in shape and size to that of the elder [saúco], a little longer, and in color and sheen resembles the leaf of the common cane.

Inside each internode there is found so much water as suffices to quench the thirst of two men; wherefore, whoever finds himself thirsty in the forest where these canes grow is wont to bore or cut open an internode and drink the water that flows from it, which is not only good and pleasant to the taste, but medicinal and useful against kidney stones [la piedra]. Each of these internodes holds a large pitcher [cangilón] of water, and in the province of Guayaquil they serve as water jars [cántaros] with which people fetch water from the spring. The guaduas are so tall that they equal and surpass the tallest trees of the forest; and thus, when they are cut for buildings and other uses, after removing more than half that is of no service—part toward the top and part toward the trunk—they remain so long that each one measures thirty to forty feet; and the tips of those so cut are two handspans thick.

These canes serve as beams [vigas] in modest buildings, and the splits or strips taken from them serve as crosspieces [travesanos]; and being so long and light, they are commonly used to make the scaffolding [andamios] for building works. When a structure has risen very high, the canes are lashed together and, driven into the ground, they hold the scaffolding upright no matter how high the construction rises—as I witnessed in this city of Lima when the towers of the Cathedral were being built, where canes driven into the ground and joined one to another supported the scaffolding at the summit of the towers. Very tall and light ladders are also made from two of these canes, with rungs and crosspieces of strips four fingers wide cut from the same canes. On two of these canes rests a litter [litera] carried by two mules, and in these litters one travels in great comfort across the plains [Llanos] and sandy wastes of this kingdom from the port of Payta to this city of Lima—a distance of two hundred leagues—and from here to the port of Arica, another two hundred. In the diocese of Quito, which encompasses the province of Guayaquil, this plant is called guadua; in the Quechua [quichua] language, mamac; and in Aymara [aimará] it has two names: tupa and tocara.

CHAPTER LXXXIII
On the Ipa

The ipa is a species of cane somewhat thinner than the guadua, but solid [maciza] and very tough; it grows in moist ground, as does the guadua, and reaches five or six estados in height. The largest are as thick as a man's arm, and the thinnest as thick as the thumb; they are thicker at the nodes than at the middle of the internodes. From each node they put forth a very dense bundle of branchlets as slender as wheat stalks, up to two handspans long, with many leaves that resemble those of the guadua, though a little narrower. When dry, these canes are brownish [pardiscas], and when green they are very tough and flexible; so much so that under the weight of their crown and branches they bend and bow until their shoots touch the ground, interlacing with one another in such a way that they form graceful arches in the forest where they grow, which appear to have been made by human craft. They grow so densely that one cannot pass through them. They are so pleasing to the eye that people commonly bring them green, with their branches and leaves, to the towns, in order to deck the streets and make arches through which processions pass on solemn feast days. In the Quechua language this cane is called ipa, and in Aymara curcuma, and also chinchiru.

There is found another variety of these solid canes as thick as the guaduas; for I recall having seen on one occasion a cane of this kind serving as a gangplank [puente] to pass from the shore to a frigate [fragata] that lay anchored close alongside it.

entities_mentioned.name: guadgua (caña de Guayaquil; quichua: mamac; aimará: tupa, tocara)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Guadua angustifolia Kunth (Guadua bamboo)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description—circumference up to three handspans, internodes holding large quantities of water, thorny branches, cypress-like crown, native to the Guayaquil region and traded to Lima—matches Guadua angustifolia precisely. The water found inside the internodes is described as medicinal against kidney stones [la piedra]; no preparation method is given beyond boring or cutting open an internode and drinking the liquid directly.

entities_mentioned.name: ipa (aimará: curcuma / chinchiru)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chusquea species (likely Chusquea scandens or related Andean Chusquea sp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as solid (non-hollow), thinner and more flexible than the guadua, growing in moist ground, bending under its own weight to form arches, and used for street decorations. The genus Chusquea comprises solid-stemmed bamboos native to the Andes and fits this description well, though a more precise species identification is uncertain. No medicinal use is attributed to the ipa in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: saúco (elder, used as leaf-size comparator)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus nigra L. or a related Sambucus sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a morphological comparator for the guadua leaf.

results.translator_notes:
1. 'Guadgua' throughout is the standard colonial-era spelling of what is today universally written 'guadua'; the modern botanical genus name Guadua derives from this word. I have preserved 'guadua' in the translation as the recognizable English/scientific form while keeping the original spelling in the entity list.
2. OCR error 'nuielias' (in 'las nuielias ramas') silently corrected to 'muchas'; 'coija' corrected to 'copa'; 'cana' (caña de Guayaquil heading) corrected to 'caña'; 'tierra yinca' corrected to 'tierra húmeda' (context makes 'yinca' an obvious OCR error for 'húmeda', as guadua is repeatedly described as a moisture-loving plant); 'fregata … surta' corrected to 'fragata … surta'.
3. 'La piedra' (kidney/bladder stones) is a standard period medical term; the preparation method stated is simply to bore or cut an internode and drink the water that flows from it—no further processing described.
4. Measurements: palmo ≈ ~8 in / 21 cm; codo (cubit) ≈ ~18 in / 45 cm; estado ≈ ~5.5–6 ft / ~1.7 m; legua (league) ≈ ~3.5 miles / ~5.5 km.
5. The Aymara name 'curcuma' for the ipa is a phonetic transcription and has no relation to the spice turmeric (Curcuma longa).

results.title_inferred: Chapter XVI: On the Gaguey and Related New World Fig-Trees
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XVI
Del gaguey

Algunas frutas lleva esta tierra que corresponden a nuestros higos de Europa, sin embargo que son de muy distinto género, de los cuales hallamos cinco o seis especies diferentes: la primera es la del gaguey, que es un árbol así llamado en la lengua de la isla Española, y nómbranlo los españoles higuera de las Indias, por la semejanza que tiene su fruta con los higos verdaderos. Es árbol grande como un moral, muy alto, de mucha hoja, muy copado porque produce las ramas muy parejas. Su hoja es como la del nogal, salvo que es más lisa, llana y tiesa y más ancha hacia la punta que por en medio. Tiene una propiedad este árbol en que se parece mucho a la higuera, y es que, partiendo un ramo dél o arrancando una hoja, destila leche tan blanca y pegajosa como la de la higuera; y lo mismo acaece rompiendo la hoja, que de las venillas della brota esta leche. La fruta es pequeña y ahusada, muy parecida en el tamaño y forma a serbas; cuando está madura, es en lo exterior amarilla, tiene la cáscara semejante a la de un higo seco antes de sazonar, que llamamos añublados; dentro, está llena de unos granitos menuditos como los del higo, al cual también se parece en el sabor, aunque es un poco más seca. Es fruta silvestre y de poca estima. Echa este árbol por todo el tronco muchas raíces delgadas, poco más gruesas que un dedo, que lo van ciñendo alrededor y cruzando una sobre otra, con que parece estar el pie del árbol cubierto de una red tejida destas raíces, las cuales se acaban antes de llegar al suelo. De la corteza deste árbol se hacen en algunas partes sogas y alpargates, a falta de cáñamo.

La segunda especie de higueras desta tierra se llama civucan en la misma isla Española; es árbol grande, vistoso y fresco; la hoja, parecida a la del sauce y de buena madera para labrar. Echa una fruta como avellanas blancas, y dentro tiene menudísimos granitos, que parecen liendres. La fruta es dulce y de buen sabor, y llámanla algunos la puta de las liendres.

A otra especie de higueras llaman amacoztic en la Nueva España; nace abrazada con las piedras como lo significa su nombre; es árbol grande, con las hojas anchas como de yedra, redondas y gruesas, tirantes a purpúreas y casi de figura de corazón; la corteza, verde, tirante a amarilla y roja; el tronco, liso y como de higuera; y echa la fruta pegada a los mismos troncos; la cual es semejante a higos pequeños de color purpúreo y está llena de simiente menuda y roja. La leche deste árbol es buena para curar llagas, y el cocimiento de sus raíces, para los dolores de pecho.

Otra casta de higuera nace en la diócesis de Guajaca, en la Nueva España, y allí la llaman hingili; es árbol mediano y su hoja, como de almendro, algo menor; la fruta es del tamaño de una guinda, algo chata y con un ojito como de higo; cuando está madura se pone morada y tiene dentro unos granillos como de higo, y el sabor, dulce, parecido al del higo.

Otra especie de higos se halla en la provincia de Soconusco, diócesis de Chiapa, que son los mayores que yo he visto. Son tan grandes como higos doñigales y de la misma forma, verdes en lo exterior, y dentro, llenos de unos granillos colorados; pero son estos higos muy tiesos y secos. El árbol que los da es muy crecido; hace la hoja como adelfa; tiene la corteza delgada y tierna, y esparce sus ramas a la redonda, las cuales, con el tronco, están llenas de hormigueros, que por cualquiera parte que los rompan se hallan dentro llenas de hormigas.

En la misma provincia de Soconusco nace otro árbol que también lleva higos algo menores que los precedentes; pero conviene advertir, que todas las frutas que llaman higos de las Indias son muy agretes y groseras y que no son dignas de compararse con la excelencia y suavidad de nuestros higos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XVI
On the Gaguey

This land bears certain fruits that correspond to our European figs, though they are of a very different genus, of which we find five or six distinct species. The first is that of the gaguey, a tree known by that name in the language of the island of Hispaniola, and called by the Spaniards higuera de las Indias [Indian fig-tree] on account of the resemblance its fruit bears to true figs. It is a large tree like a mulberry, very tall, abundantly leafed, and very spreading because it produces its branches in a very even manner. Its leaf is like that of the walnut, except that it is smoother, flatter, and stiffer, and broader toward the tip than in the middle. This tree has one property in which it closely resembles the fig tree: namely, that upon breaking off a branch or tearing away a leaf, it exudes a milk as white and sticky as that of the fig tree; and the same occurs when the leaf is broken, for from its small veins this milk flows out. The fruit is small and spindle-shaped, very similar in size and form to service-berries [serbas]; when ripe it is yellow on the outside, with a skin resembling that of a dried fig before it is fully cured — what we call añublados [blight-dried figs]; inside, it is full of tiny seeds like those of a fig, which it also resembles in flavor, though it is a little drier. It is a wild fruit of little esteem. The tree sends out all along its trunk many thin roots, scarcely thicker than a finger, which encircle it and cross over one another, so that the base of the tree appears to be covered with a net woven of these roots, which stop short before reaching the ground. In some places, ropes and sandals [alpargates] are made from the bark of this tree in the absence of hemp.

The second species of fig-tree of this land is called civucan on the same island of Hispaniola. It is a large, handsome, and fresh-looking tree; its leaf resembles that of the willow, and its wood is good for working. It bears a fruit like white hazelnuts, containing inside very tiny seeds resembling nits [liendres]. The fruit is sweet and of good flavor, and some call it "the nit fruit" [puta de las liendres].

Another species of fig-tree is called amacoztic in New Spain; it grows clasped around rocks, as its name signifies. It is a large tree with broad leaves like those of ivy, round, thick, inclining to purple, and nearly heart-shaped; the bark is green, tending toward yellow and red; the trunk is smooth and fig-tree-like; and it bears its fruit attached directly to the trunks themselves. The fruit resembles small figs of a purple color and is filled with tiny red seeds. The milk [latex] of this tree is good for curing sores [llagas], and a decoction [cocimiento] of its roots is used for pains of the chest.

Another kind of fig-tree grows in the diocese of Oaxaca [Guajaca], in New Spain, where it is called hingili. It is a medium-sized tree, with a leaf like that of the almond, somewhat smaller. The fruit is the size of a cherry [guinda], somewhat flat, with a small eye like that of a fig; when ripe it turns purple and contains inside small seeds like those of a fig, with a sweet flavor resembling that of the fig.

Another species of fig is found in the province of Soconusco, diocese of Chiapa, and these are the largest I have ever seen. They are as large as donegal figs [higos doñigales] and of the same form, green on the outside, and inside filled with small reddish seeds; however, these figs are very tough and dry. The tree that bears them is very tall; its leaf resembles that of the oleander [adelfa]; its bark is thin and tender, and it spreads its branches all around; these branches, along with the trunk, are riddled with ant colonies, so that wherever they are broken open, they are found to be full of ants.

In the same province of Soconusco there grows another tree that likewise bears figs somewhat smaller than the foregoing. It should be noted, however, that all the fruits called figs of the Indies are very tart and coarse, and are not worthy of comparison with the excellence and delicacy of our own figs.

'añublados' (blight-dried or mildewed figs) is a period-specific term for figs that have dried without fully ripening — preserved in brackets in translation. 3. 'puta de las liendres' is a vulgar colloquial name meaning roughly 'nit-whore' or 'nit-fruit'; translated descriptively as 'nit fruit' to avoid confusion while flagging the original. 4. 'higos doñigales' refers to a large variety of Spanish fig (Ficus carica 'Dottato' or similar), used as a size comparison. 5. 'amacoztic' is Nahuatl: 'ama-' (paper/fig-bark tree) + 'coztic' (yellow), consistent with a yellow-barked Ficus. 6. The medical uses extracted from 'amacoztic' are the only formal materia medica entries in this chapter. 7. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'qpie' → 'que', 'qon' → 'con', 'dél' normalized, 'clorteza' → 'corteza', 'cánamo' → 'cáñamo', 'BU' → 'su', 'v' → 'y' (conjunction), 'i)recedenleg' → 'precedentes', and closing fragment restored from context.

CHAPTER XXII
On the Ibapuru

In the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra, in this kingdom of Peru, they call ibapuru a certain fruit whose tree is of the size of an olive tree, with small leaves and sparsely covered with them; its fruit is black, in the shape and size of the sloe-berries [endrinas] of Spain; it contains three or four small seeds inside, like grapes, which—[TEXT BREAKS OFF]

entities_mentioned.name: ibapuru
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrciaria cauliflora (jabuticaba) or a closely related Plinia/Myrciaria species
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'ibapuru' is of Guaraní origin and is associated with jabuticaba-type fruits in the Santa Cruz de la Sierra region of present-day Bolivia. The description of a black fruit resembling a sloe-berry with multiple seeds is broadly consistent with Myrciaria or Plinia species, though the segment is truncated and no medicinal use is recorded here.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus spinosa (blackthorn / sloe berry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a familiar European comparator for the size and color of the ibapuru fruit.

results.translator_notes: The segment is truncated mid-sentence ('que se'), indicating the OCR or transcription source is incomplete. No medicinal use, ailment, or preparation method is described in the surviving text. The geographic reference to 'este reino del Perú' reflects the broader colonial administrative usage of 'Peru,' which at the time encompassed much of Spanish South America including present-day Bolivia.

results.title_inferred: The Mamón of Cartagena
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIII
Del mamón de Cartagena

En la provincia de Cartagena se da una fruta que allí nombran mamón; es del tamaño de un limón ceutí, redonda, verde por de fuera; la cáscara, delgada y correosa como de limón; en el interior, está llena de una sustancia muy aguanosa, como la de las uvas, de sabor apetitoso, agridulce, que se come chupándola. Los huesecillos son unas pepitas blancas y redondas del tamaño de avellanas, que se comen tostadas y tienen sabor de bellotas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIII
Of the Mamón of Cartagena

In the province of Cartagena there grows a fruit that is locally called mamón. It is the size of a Ceuta lemon [a small variety of lemon], round, and green on the outside; the rind is thin and leathery like that of a lemon. Inside, it is filled with a very watery pulp, similar to that of grapes, with an appealing bittersweet flavor, which is eaten by sucking it. The small seeds [huesecillos] are little white, round kernels the size of hazelnuts, which are eaten toasted and have the flavor of acorns.

entities_mentioned.name: mamón de Cartagena
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Melicoccus bijugatus (Spanish lime / mamoncillo)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description closely matches Melicoccus bijugatus: small, round, green-skinned fruit with thin leathery rind, watery translucent pulp eaten by sucking, and relatively large seeds. Widely known as mamoncillo, quenepa, or genip throughout the Caribbean and northern South America. results.title_inferred: On the Capulíes and Other Cherry-like Fruits of the Indies
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIX
De los capulíes

Asimismo hállanse en esta tierra algunas frutas que se parecen a nuestras guindas y cerezas; por lo cual solemos llamarlas cerezas de las Indias. La fruta deste género que tiene mayor semejanza y parentesco con nuestras cerezas son los capulíes. El árbol es de mediana grandeza, derecho, con ramas largas; las hojas como las del almendro, medianamente aserradas; la flor, blanca, pequeña, de cinco hojitas sutiles pendientes en racimos, de las cuales procede la fruta, que son unos granos muy semejantes a las cerezas en el tamaño, color y forma y también en sus pepitas y sabor, sino que tira tanto cuanto al de moras de zarza. Nace cada grano de por sí de un pezoncillo muy corto que es en lo que más se diferencia el capulí de nuestras cerezas, si bien tampoco se iguala a ellas en el sabor. Los indios suelen hacer vino desta fruta.

El polvo de la corteza del árbol hace las nubes de los ojos y aclara la vista. Su madera es colorada y buena de labrar, y della se hacen cajas de arcabuces. Nace este árbol en tierras templadas, como lo es la comarca de México, y dase ya en esta ciudad de Lima, adonde se trujo pocos años ha de la Nueva España.

Otra casta de cerezas de la tierra da en esta ciudad de Lima, y las vi primero en la isla Española. El árbol es de la grandeza de un granado y muy parecido a él en su talle y hoja. La fruta es de hechura de cerezas, muy colorada y un poquito mayor que las cerezas y no de tan buen sabor; tiene dentro dos o tres pepitas larguillas diferentes de las cerezas; no es fruta ésta de que hace caso. La macagua es otra especie de cerezas; llámase así en lengua de la isla Española; el árbol es grande y parecido al nogal en su grandeza y hoja; la fruta es como aceitunas pequeñas, pero el sabor es como el de las cerezas. La madera deste árbol es muy buena de labrar y la hoja muy vistosa y verde.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIX
On the Capulíes

Likewise, in this land one finds certain fruits that resemble our sour cherries [guindas] and sweet cherries [cerezas]; for which reason we commonly call them cherries of the Indies. The fruit of this kind that most closely resembles and is most akin to our cherries is the capulí. The tree is of middling size, upright, with long branches; the leaves are like those of the almond tree, moderately serrated; the flower is white and small, consisting of five slender little petals hanging in clusters, from which the fruit proceeds—these are small berries very similar to cherries in size, color, and form, and also in their pits and flavor, except that the taste leans somewhat toward that of blackberries. Each berry grows individually on a very short stem, which is the feature that most distinguishes the capulí from our cherries, though it likewise does not equal them in flavor. The Indians commonly make wine from this fruit.

The powder of the bark of the tree removes cloudiness from the eyes [cataracts / corneal opacities] and clears the sight. Its wood is reddish and easy to work, and from it are made the stocks of arquebuses [firearms]. This tree grows in temperate lands, as is the district of Mexico, and it is now cultivated in this city of Lima, whither it was brought a few years ago from New Spain.

Another kind of cherry of this land grows in this city of Lima; I first saw them on the island of Hispaniola. The tree is the size of a pomegranate tree and very similar to it in shape and leaf. The fruit is shaped like cherries, very red and a little larger than cherries, though not of as good a flavor; it contains two or three somewhat elongated pits unlike those of cherries; it is not a fruit held in much esteem. The macagua is another species of cherry; it is called thus in the language of the island of Hispaniola. The tree is large and similar to the walnut in its size and leaf; the fruit is like small olives, but the flavor is like that of cherries. The wood of this tree is very good for working, and the leaf is very handsome and green.

entities_mentioned.name: capulí
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus serotina subsp. capuli (Capulin cherry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to Mexico and Central America; widely noted in colonial sources. Cobo describes bark powder used medicinally to treat eye cloudiness (likely corneal opacity or cataracts). Wine was also made from the fruit. The short peduncle is noted as the key morphological distinction from European cherries.

entities_mentioned.name: cerezas de la tierra (unnamed species, first seen in Hispaniola)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Malpighia emarginata (acerola / Barbados cherry) or a related Malpighiaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as resembling a pomegranate tree in stature and leaf, with very red fruit slightly larger than a cherry and containing two or three elongated pits. Not held in high culinary esteem. Multiple seeds suggest it is not Prunus.

entities_mentioned.name: macagua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Chrysobalanus icaco (coco plum) or an unidentified Antillean species
entities_mentioned.notes: Name said to come from the language of Hispaniola (Taíno). Described as a large tree similar to the walnut in size and leaf, with fruit like small olives but cherry-flavored. Wood noted as excellent for working. Identification is uncertain; the Taíno name 'macagua' has also been applied to other Caribbean plants.

results.title_inferred: On the Tempesquisti and the Manzanilla (Manchineel Tree)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXI

Del tempesquisti

El tempesquisti es un árbol grande y hermoso con los extremos de las ramas muy pobladas de hoja, la cual es reluciente, en lo cual y en el pezón de la misma hoja es muy parecida a la del peral, salvo que es más larga y puntiaguda. Esparcen estas hojas en las iglesias, por adorno, en lugar de juncias. Produce este árbol una fruta negra y dulce como higo, que se come.

CAPITULO XXXII

De la manzanilla

En muchas costas destas Indias nace cierto árbol, cuya fruta llaman los españoles manzanilla, por ser parecida a una manzana pequeña. El árbol es bajo como dos o tres estados, muy copado y hermoso al parecer, y con la hoja como la del peral. Echa una fruta como cermeñas, sólo que es redonda y matizada un poquito de rojo, que la hace más agraciada. Da de sí un olor suave, de suerte que caminando por donde hay esta fruta, a gran distancia se conoce por su fragancia. Pero es fruta muy ponzoñosa, a cuya causa la echan los indios en la yerba venenosa que hacen para untar las flechas. Algunos españoles recién llegados de España, llevados de su buen olor y vista, sin conocer su maligna calidad, la han comido y estado muy a peligro de morir.

El pescado y marisco que se cría en las riberas de la mar donde se da esta manzanilla, suelen comer délla, por lo cual es malsano. La sombra deste manzano, a quien duerme en ella, causa hinchazón y calenturas, que algunas veces matan; y el humo de su leña causa penosos y largos dolores de cabeza.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXI

On the Tempesquisti

The tempesquisti is a large and handsome tree whose branch tips are very densely covered with leaves. The leaf is shiny, and in this respect—as well as in its petiole [leaf-stalk]—it greatly resembles that of the pear tree, except that it is longer and more pointed. These leaves are strewn in churches as decoration, in place of rushes [juncias]. The tree produces a black, sweet fruit resembling a fig, which is eaten.

CHAPTER XXXII

On the Manzanilla

On many coasts of these Indies there grows a certain tree whose fruit the Spaniards call manzanilla [little apple], because it resembles a small apple. The tree stands low, about two or three fathoms [estados] in height, very leafy and handsome in appearance, with a leaf like that of the pear tree. It bears a fruit resembling a bergamot pear [cermeña], except that it is round and tinged with a little red, which makes it all the more attractive. It emits a pleasant fragrance, such that when walking through a place where this fruit grows, one can detect it by its scent from a great distance. However, it is a highly poisonous fruit, and for this reason the Indians add it to the venomous preparation they make for smearing on their arrows. Some Spaniards newly arrived from Spain, drawn in by its pleasant smell and appearance, and not knowing its malignant nature, have eaten it and come very close to dying.

The fish and shellfish that breed along the shores of the sea where this manzanilla grows are accustomed to feed on it, and for this reason they are unwholesome to eat. The shade of this tree, for anyone who sleeps beneath it, causes swelling and fevers, which sometimes prove fatal; and the smoke from its wood causes painful and prolonged headaches.

entities_mentioned.name: tempesquisti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crataegus mexicana or possibly Licania platypus (sansapote); identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a large tree with shiny, pear-shaped leaves used as church floor-strewing herbs, bearing sweet black edible fruit resembling a fig. The name 'tempesquisti' is of Nahuatl origin. Some sources associate it with Licania platypus or related Chrysobalanaceae; remains uncertain without further context.

entities_mentioned.name: manzanilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hippomane mancinella (manchineel tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: All described properties—attractive apple-like toxic fruit, poisonous sap used on arrow tips, toxic shade causing swelling and fever, toxic smoke from burning wood, contamination of coastal seafood—are classic attributes of the manchineel (Hippomane mancinella, family Euphorbiaceae), one of the most toxic trees in the Americas. The fruit is described as resembling a small apple (hence 'manzanilla'), round, tinged with red, and sweetly fragrant. No medicinal preparation described; its toxicity is the focus. Used by indigenous peoples as an arrow poison ingredient.

results.title_inferred: The Tucuñero Tree and Its Fruit
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIV

Del tucuñero

En la misma provincia de Santa Cruz nace un árbol que llaman tucuñero. Da una fruta amarilla del tamaño de limones, en racimos de tres en tres y de cuatro en cuatro; produce la semilla fuera, encima de la fruta. Es el tucuñero fruta dulce con un poquito de agrio, y pasada es tan dulce como la miel y se guarda de un año para otro.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIV

On the Tucuñero

In the same province of Santa Cruz there grows a tree they call the tucuñero. It bears a yellow fruit the size of lemons, in clusters of three or four; it produces its seed on the outside, on top of the fruit. The tucuñero fruit is sweet with a slight tartness, and when dried [pasada] it is as sweet as honey and can be kept from one year to the next.

entities_mentioned.name: tucuñero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Spondias mombin (yellow mombin / jocote) or a related Anacardiaceae species; the external seed placement may suggest a Cashew-family relative or possibly a species of Bactris or related palm — however the cluster arrangement and lemon-sized yellow fruit with an exocarpic seed is highly suggestive of the cajá or related Spondias. Alternatively may refer to a species of Acrocomia or Attalea palm of the Santa Cruz (Bolivia) region.
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of the seed produced 'outside, on top of the fruit' is a notable morphological detail reminiscent of the cashew (Anacardium occidentale), where the true seed sits atop a swollen peduncle (cashew apple). However, the cluster arrangement and lemon-size argue against cashew. No medical use is described in this segment. Province of Santa Cruz refers to present-day Santa Cruz de la Sierra region, Bolivia.

results.translator_notes: The OCR rendered 'tiicuñero' in the chapter heading, which is clearly a double-i OCR error for 'tucuñero'; silently corrected in the cleaned text. The word 'pasada' in context means dried or overripe/preserved fruit rather than simply 'past'; translated as 'dried' with the original term noted in brackets. No medicinal or pharmaceutical content is present in this segment — it is purely a botanical and organoleptic description.

results.title_inferred: On Achiote (Annatto): Tree Description, Preparation, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLVIII
Del achiote

En la Nueva España llaman achime a una pasta o panecillos colorados que hacen de las pepitas de cierto árbol y sirve para dar color al chocolate y a los guisados. El árbol es de la grandeza de un naranjo mediano, copado y de agradable parecer; la hoja, como de nogal, sólo que es angosta y larga; la fruta que lleva es un erizo del tamaño de una nuez, muy semejante a una almeja, áspero por fuera, por estar cubierto de unas espinillas blandas que no punzan, de color pardo después de seco, con una punta pequeña en el remate como la de la nuez; la cáscara es más blanda que la de la adormidera; tiene dentro unas pepitas muy rojas del tamaño de granillos de uvas, muy redondas. Nace este árbol en tierra caliente, y donde se hace granjería de sus frutos es en las costas de la mar del Sur de la Nueva España. Benefícianle de esta manera: echan en remojo estos granillos hasta que, lavándolos, se les despega aquella sustancia colorada de que estaban cubiertos, y ellos quedan blancos, parecidos a los yeros; el agua que quedó teñida de su color, la ponen a cocer al fuego hasta darle su punto, y que la sustancia pingüe del fruto suba arriba como espuma, la cual van recogiendo con una cuchara; cuelan luego y se exprime en un paño, en el cual queda lo espeso que es el achiote, que amasado en bollos o panecillos, lo ponen a secar al sol; y éste es el achiote tan estimado en la Nueva España. Con él solían los indios untarse el cuerpo, que ellos llaman embijarse, y por eso en algunas partes llaman bija a esta planta. Su madera es útil para fuego, pegando un palo con otro, y la corteza es buena para hacer sogas. Es el achiote acomodado para los pintores. Y no es menos provechoso para el uso de la medicina, porque cura las cámaras de sangre, provoca la orina y mitiga la sed. Es tan tenaz este color, que ni con jabón ni lejía se limpia el lienzo que con él se tiñe.

(Footnote 4: I doubt that bija and embijarse are words of Indian origin, because I have read in some manuscript predating the discovery of the New World the phrase "color bixio" meaning a reddish-purple or violet color.)
(Footnote 5: [continued])
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLVIII
On Achiote

In New Spain they call achime [achiote] a paste or small reddish cakes made from the seeds of a certain tree, which serves to give color to chocolate and to stews. The tree is of the size of a medium orange tree, wide-crowned and of pleasant appearance; the leaf is like that of a walnut tree, except that it is narrow and long; the fruit it bears is a burr the size of a walnut, very similar to a mussel shell, rough on the outside from being covered with soft little spines that do not prick, brownish in color once dry, with a small point at the tip like that of a walnut; the husk is softer than that of the poppy; inside it has very red seeds the size of small grape pips, quite round. This tree grows in hot country, and where commerce is made of its fruits is along the coasts of the South Sea [Pacific coast] of New Spain. They prepare it in the following manner: they soak these little seeds until, by washing them, the red substance with which they were covered comes away, leaving them white and resembling bitter vetches [yeros]; the water that has been dyed with this color is then set to boil over a fire until it reaches the right consistency and the fatty substance of the fruit rises to the top like foam, which is skimmed off with a spoon; it is then strained and pressed in a cloth, in which the thick matter remains — this is the achiote; kneaded into balls or small cakes, it is set to dry in the sun, and this is the achiote so prized in New Spain. The Indians used to anoint their bodies with it, which they call embijarse [to bija oneself], and for this reason in some places the plant is called bija. Its wood is useful for making fire by rubbing one stick against another, and its bark is good for making ropes. Achiote is suitable for painters. And it is no less beneficial for medicinal use, because it cures bloody flux [dysentery with blood], promotes urination, and relieves thirst. This color is so tenacious that neither soap nor lye will clean cloth that has been dyed with it.

(Footnote 4: I doubt that bija and embijarse are words of Indian origin, because I have read in some manuscript predating the discovery of the New World the phrase "color bixio" meaning a reddish-purple or violet color.)

entities_mentioned.name: achiote / achime / bija
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bixa orellana L. (annatto)
entities_mentioned.notes: Known today as annatto; source of the natural dye bixin/norbixin. Cobo notes regional name variation between 'achiote' (New Spain) and 'bija' (other areas). Author questions whether 'bija/embijarse' are Amerindian loanwords, citing a pre-Columbian manuscript using 'color bixio' for a reddish-purple hue.

entities_mentioned.name: achiote (medicinal use)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bixa orellana L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Conditions treated: cámaras de sangre [bloody dysentery/bloody flux], urinary retention (provokes urination), and excessive thirst. Preparation: seeds soaked in water, the colored liquid boiled, the fatty foam skimmed, strained through cloth, the resulting thick paste formed into cakes and sun-dried.

results.title_inferred: The Eloxochitl (Magnolia or similar fragrant flower)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXVI

Del eloxochitl

Esta es una de las flores de mayor fragancia de las Indias. Es del tamaño y talle de una mazorca de maíz; eso quiere decir, en mexicano, eloxochitl, flor de elote o mazorca de maíz, que todo es uno. Compónese de muchas hojas o túnicas, todas cerradas unas sobre otras, y tan ancha cada una, que entre dos abrazan el ámbito de la flor, y tan largas como la misma flor. Las exteriores son verdes tirantes a amarillo, y las interiores de un color entre amarillo y blanco. Cuando la flor está de sazón, se abren un poco por las puntas sus hojas, y basta una flor déstas en un aposento para que esté oloroso.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXVI

On the Eloxochitl

This is one of the most fragrant flowers of the Indies. It is the size and shape of an ear of maize; and that is precisely what the word means in Mexican [Nahuatl]: eloxochitl, the flower of the elote, or ear of maize—all one and the same thing. It is composed of many petals or tunics [túnicas], all closed one over another, each so broad that two of them together encompass the full circumference of the flower, and as long as the flower itself. The outer ones are green tending toward yellow, and the inner ones are a color between yellow and white. When the flower is in season, its petals open a little at the tips, and a single one of these flowers placed in a room is sufficient to fill it with fragrance.

entities_mentioned.name: eloxochitl
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Magnolia mexicana (Mexican magnolia) or possibly Talauma mexicana; the Nahuatl name 'eloxochitl' (ear-of-corn flower) is historically associated with species in the Magnoliaceae family native to Mexico
entities_mentioned.notes: The Nahuatl term 'eloxochitl' literally means 'elote-flower' (flower resembling a maize ear). Cobo does not describe medicinal use in this passage; the focus is entirely on the flower's remarkable fragrance and morphology. No preparation method or ailment is mentioned.

results.title_inferred: On Balsam: Three or Four Species of Balsam-Yielding Trees in the Indies
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXII
Del bálsamo

El árbol que destila el bálsamo en estas Indias no de sola una especie, sino de tres o cuatro. Es este licor muy semejante al bálsamo de Siria, y no inferior a él en olor y facultades. La una especie de árboles que lo dan y de mayor grandeza, nace en la diócesis de Guatemala, donde yo lo vi, y en otras tierras calientes; crece más que un moral, y hace un tronco grueso y de madera olorosa, y tan recia, que sirve en los usos que requieren madera muy fuerte, como es para ejes de ingenios de azúcar y otros semejantes. Las hojas son como de almendro, algo mayores y más redondas y agudas; las flores, amarillas en los extremos de los ramos, al principio en forma de largos bolsillos, con cierta simiente blanca que inclina a color amarillo. Sajado el tronco deste árbol, destila el licor que llamamos bálsamo, de color de arrope, rojo tirante a negro, de sabor agudo y algo amargo, de olor vehemente, pero gratísimo. Sácase también este mismo licor de otra manera, que es cociendo en agua los renuevos y ramos tiernos picados menudamente, y cogiendo con un vidrio el licor que nada sobre el agua. No es tan bueno este segundo como el primero, pero el uno y otro son buenos para sahumerios y para curar infinitas enfermedades. De su semilla se saca también un aceite muy provechoso.

El segundo árbol que destila bálsamo es de mediana grandeza; el tronco, no más grueso que el muslo, de madera sólida y olorosa; tiene las hojas poco mayores que un real; la flor es pequeña y blanca; la fruta, semejante a las bayas del laurel. Sácase el licor deste árbol de sus cortezas remojadas por vía de destilación.

El árbol de que sacan el bálsamo en la isla Española se dice goaconax; es del grandor y hechura de un granado, muy agradable al parecer; tiene uno, dos y tres pies como el granado, y en la hoja también se le parece, salvo que la tiene menor; pareciendo en el tronco que está seco, y las hojas que están verdes: no hace copa, sino que las ramas suben derechas cada una por sí. Alumbra su madera como tea y da buen olor de sí. Sácase el bálsamo por infusión, sajando el tronco del árbol, y también cociendo y exprimiendo las hojas.

En el pueblo de Tolú, diócesis de Cartagena, se saca también bálsamo de un árbol de la grandeza de un granado; y este bálsamo y el primero, son los más preciados, si bien difieren en que el primero es líquido como arrope, y éste de Cartagena cuajado y duro, que se muele en polvos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXII
On Balsam

The tree that yields balsam in these Indies is not of a single species, but of three or four. This liquor is very similar to the balsam of Syria, and in no way inferior to it in fragrance or properties. One species—the largest of those that produce it—grows in the diocese of Guatemala, where I myself saw it, and in other warm lands. It grows taller than a mulberry tree and forms a thick trunk of fragrant and very hard wood, so strong that it serves for purposes requiring very sturdy timber, such as axles for sugar mills and similar uses. The leaves are like those of the almond tree, somewhat larger, rounder, and more pointed; the flowers are yellow at the tips of the branches, appearing at first in the form of long pouches [bolsillos], with certain seeds that are white tending toward yellow. When the trunk of this tree is scored [sajado], it exudes the liquor we call balsam: the color of grape syrup [arrope], red verging on black, of sharp and somewhat bitter taste, of a powerful but most pleasing scent. This same liquor is also extracted by another method, namely by boiling in water the finely chopped new shoots and tender branches, and collecting with a glass vessel the liquor that floats on the surface of the water. This second product is not as good as the first, but both are useful for fumigations [sahumerios] and for treating a great many ailments. From its seeds, a very beneficial oil is also extracted.

The second tree that yields balsam is of medium size; the trunk is no thicker than a thigh, with solid and fragrant wood; its leaves are slightly larger than a real [a silver coin]; the flower is small and white; the fruit is similar to laurel berries. The liquor of this tree is extracted from its soaked bark by means of distillation.

The tree from which balsam is drawn on the island of Hispaniola is called goaconax; it is the size and shape of a pomegranate tree, and quite pleasing in appearance. It has one, two, or three trunks like the pomegranate, and its leaf also resembles that of the pomegranate, except that it is smaller. The trunk appears dry while the leaves remain green; it forms no canopy, but rather the branches grow straight up, each on its own. Its wood burns brightly like a torch and gives off a pleasant scent. The balsam is extracted by incision [infusión], scoring the trunk of the tree, and also by boiling and pressing the leaves.

In the town of Tolú, in the diocese of Cartagena, balsam is likewise extracted from a tree the size of a pomegranate; and this balsam, along with the first [Guatemalan] one, are the most prized—though they differ in that the first is liquid like grape syrup, while this one from Cartagena is congealed and hard, and can be ground into powder.

entities_mentioned.name: Bálsamo (primera especie, Guatemala)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myroxylon balsamum var. pereirae (Balsam of Peru) or Myroxylon balsamum (Balsam of Tolu) — the Guatemala species most likely Myroxylon balsamum var. pereirae
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a large tree with almond-like leaves, yellow flowers, resinous heartwood used for sugar mill axles; balsam extracted by trunk-scoring or by boiling tender shoots. Used for fumigations and treating numerous ailments; oil extracted from seeds. The liquid, reddish-black balsam resembling grape syrup aligns with Balsam of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: Bálsamo (segunda especie, unnamed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Myroxylon or Protium, identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Medium-sized tree, trunk no thicker than a thigh, small white flowers, laurel-like berries. Balsam extracted from soaked bark by distillation.

entities_mentioned.name: Goaconax (Hispaniola balsam tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Bursera simaruba or a related Bursera sp., or alternatively Protium sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Indigenous Taíno name. Described as pomegranate-sized, multi-trunked, with leaves smaller than pomegranate; wood burns like a torch (resinous). Balsam extracted by scoring trunk and by boiling/pressing leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Bálsamo de Tolú (Cartagena)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myroxylon balsamum var. balsamum (Balsam of Tolu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Solid, hard balsam that can be ground into powder, distinguished from the liquid Guatemalan balsam. Produced near the town of Tolú, Diocese of Cartagena — the classic origin of Balsam of Tolu.

entities_mentioned.name: Bálsamo de Siria
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora opobalsamum (Balm of Gilead / Syrian Balsam)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative standard for quality of fragrance and medicinal properties.

results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXIII
Del pica

Este es un árbol mediano, del grandor de un olivo; su hoja, redonda, y tan grande como la palma de la mano; echa una frutilla silvestre no comestible, colorada y redonda, del tamaño de pequeñas manzanas. Destila de este árbol una goma o resina de color de oro y muy reluciente, parecida en el olor y color al incienso, aunque tira algo su olor al de la almáciga. Quémanla en lugar de incienso en las provincias del Collao, en el Perú. Nace el pica en las provincias de los Andes, y hay gran copia dellos en lo que alcanzan de temple de los Andes, las provincias de Caraguaya y la Larecaja, la primera de la diócesis del Cuzco; la segunda, de la de Chuquiabo. Sajan los indios estos árboles para destilar esta resina en mayor cantidad; sácanla a vender a las provincias comarcanas para el efecto requerido.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXIII
On the Pica

This is a medium-sized tree, as large as an olive tree; its leaf is round and as large as the palm of the hand; it bears a small wild fruit that is not edible, red and round, the size of small apples. From this tree there distils a gum or resin of a golden colour and very lustrous, resembling incense in smell and colour, although its scent tends somewhat toward that of mastic [almáciga]. It is burned in place of incense in the provinces of the Collao, in Peru. The pica grows in the provinces of the Andes, and there is a great abundance of these trees throughout the Andean zone encompassing the provinces of Caraguaya and Larecaja—the first belonging to the diocese of Cuzco, the second to that of Chuquiabo [La Paz]. The Indians score [tap] these trees in order to obtain a greater quantity of the resin; they take it out to sell in the neighbouring provinces for the aforesaid purpose.

entities_mentioned.name: pica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clusia sp. or possibly Bursera sp.; some scholars associate Andean resin-producing 'pica' trees with Clusia (Clusiaceae) or with Protium (Burseraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The golden, lustrous resin burned as incense and compared to both frankincense and mastic suggests a Burseraceae candidate such as Protium or Bursera, but the fruit and leaf description leave identification uncertain. The geographical context (Andean yungas of Caraguaya and Larecaja) may help narrow identification.

entities_mentioned.name: almáciga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus (mastic)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an Old World aromatic reference point for comparison, not as a New World plant.

entities_mentioned.name: incienso (incense)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Boswellia sacra (true frankincense)
entities_mentioned.notes: Again used as a comparative aromatic reference; the pica resin is described as a local functional substitute burned in religious/ritual contexts.

  "original_text": "CAPITULO CVI
De las demás especies de copal

La segunda especie de copal da un árbol que tiene las hojas semejantes a las del zumaque; tiene las ramas como de alas; destila su goma en menos cantidad que el cópala y es también ilíaca y hace los mismos efectos.

La tercera especie de copal es de un árbol muy alto y liso y que fácilmente se le quita la corteza; sus hojas son pequeñas, semejantes a las de la ruda, algo mayores y más largas. Echa la fruta en racimos, que cada uno está por sí; destila este árbol un licor resinoso en poca cantidad, semejante al copal en olor y color. Suelen deshacer este licor en agua y mezclarlo con pulque y así dicen que cura las cámaras de sangre.

A la cuarta especie de copal llaman montano. Es un árbol de mediana grandeza, que tiene las hojas como las del muérdago; lleva una fruta semejante a las bellotas, cuya pepita es de provecho para muchas cosas. Mana de este árbol una goma muy semejante al incienso, por lo cual lo llaman en la Nueva España a boca llena incienso, aunque algunos lo nombran anime de las Indias. La madera de este árbol es de olor suavísimo, y su sahumerio conforta el estómago, el corazón y el cerebro.

La quinta especie de copal se dice de Tototepec. Es un árbol grande, con las hojas semejantes a las del naranjo, de color verde oscuro; su goma es semejante al copal y sirve en los mismos usos.

La sexta especie de copal es un árbol mediano: tiene el tronco como inficionado de lepra y las hojas casi redondas y pequeñas, con unos granos que cuelgan a racimos muy semejantes a los de la agicanta. Son muy olorosos y pegajosos. La goma de este árbol es muy dura, blanca y algo olorosa.

La séptima especie de copal es un árbol de mediana grandeza, que destila una lágrima o especie de incienso que tira a color blanco, algo tirante a negro. Tiene este árbol las hojas puestas en orden, y son semejantes a las de la anisilla, poco mayores;
